title: Accounting Workflow Capacity Score — Where Are Your Hours Actually Leaking?
subtitle: A 7-minute diagnostic for managing partners and COOs at boutique CPA, tax, and bookkeeping firms (11–100 employees) across the US, UK, AU, and NZ. Score where intake, prep, review, client comms, and admin are bleeding partner hours.

persona_selector.text: Which best describes your firm's headcount today?

answers.label: Solo or under 10 staff (founder plus a few juniors or contractors)
Boutique: 11–25 staff (1–3 partners, starting to formalize ops)
Growing: 26–60 staff (multi-partner, defined departments, mid-market clients)
Established: 61–100+ staff (full leadership team, multi-office or multi-state)

questions.text: How quickly does a signed engagement turn into a fully provisioned workspace, folder structure, and document request list?

answers.label: 1 — A week or more, or it slips through the cracks regularly
2 — 1–3 days depending on who is available
3 — Same day, but a person clicks through 4–6 systems manually
4 — Under 2 hours, partially automated with a manual review step
5 — Under 30 minutes, mostly automated end to end

questions.text: How automated is the flow of client information from intake form into your practice management, document portal, and accounting software?

answers.label: 1 — Re-keyed by hand into every system
2 — Copy-pasted into 2–3 systems by an admin
3 — One sync works, the rest are manual
4 — Most systems auto-sync, with one human checkpoint
5 — Single source of truth flows automatically into every downstream system

questions.text: How consistently are clients sent (and reminded about) their document request list without a person manually nudging?

answers.label: 1 — Staff manually email reminders when they remember
2 — Initial list is templated, follow-ups are ad hoc
3 — Scheduled reminders go out, but tracking who replied is manual
4 — Automated reminders plus dashboard of outstanding items
5 — Fully automated chase sequence with status visible to client and team in real time

low.headline: Onboarding is your hidden capacity sink
low.text: Every new client costs you 3–6 partner hours before any real work starts. That is not a tooling problem, it is a structured-input problem. The fastest fix is a single intake form that becomes the source of truth for every downstream system.
steps: Map every system that needs a new client record (PM, portal, GL, CRM, billing) and pick one as the master
Replace email and PDF intake with a structured form (Typeform, Jotform, or your portal's native form) that captures every field downstream systems require
Wire one automation that provisions the workspace, folders, and document request list the moment the engagement is signed

mid.headline: Intake works, but a person is still the glue
mid.text: You have templates and a process, but a human is clicking through 4–6 systems on every new client. That is 60–90 minutes per onboarding, every time. Move the glue from a person to a workflow.
steps: Audit one full onboarding end to end and time every manual click — the result is your automation backlog
Replace the human-glue steps with an n8n flow triggered by engagement signature in your PM system
Add automated document request reminders with response tracking so nobody chases by hand

high.headline: Keep sharpening — the frontier is pre-engagement triage
high.text: Your onboarding is tight. The next leak is upstream: which prospects should you have said no to before they hit intake? Top-decile firms triage clients before they consume hours.
steps: Add a structured pre-engagement scoring step that flags fit, complexity, and capacity load before you sign
Auto-route low-fit prospects to a referral partner instead of consuming a partner discovery call
Tag every signed client with predicted hours so capacity planning starts at engagement, not at delivery

questions.text: How consistently does every preparer follow the same workpaper structure and prep procedures across the firm?

answers.label: 1 — Every preparer has their own way of doing it
2 — Templates exist but adherence is uneven
3 — Standards are documented, partners enforce them per engagement
4 — Templates plus checklists are mandatory and audited
5 — Standardized, version-controlled, and the system rejects non-compliant workpapers

questions.text: How automated is data extraction from source documents (bank statements, invoices, K-1s, brokerage statements) into your working files?

answers.label: 1 — Manually keyed by staff
2 — OCR for some documents, manual for the rest
3 — OCR plus a human reconciliation pass on every doc
4 — AI extraction with exception-only human review
5 — Automated extraction, classification, and direct posting with confidence scoring

questions.text: How much partner or manager time per week is spent chasing missing client documents?

answers.label: 1 — Multiple hours a week, it is a constant fire
2 — A few hours, mostly during busy season
3 — Admin handles most of it, partners step in for stuck cases
4 — Automated reminders cover 80%+, humans handle escalations
5 — Almost zero — the system chases, escalates, and surfaces blockers

questions.text: How automated is your prior-year rollforward and anomaly detection at the start of an engagement?

answers.label: 1 — Manual rebuild every year
2 — Templates roll forward, but anomalies are caught only at review
3 — Rollforward is automated, variance analysis is manual
4 — Automated rollforward plus flagged variances against prior year
5 — System auto-flags anomalies, missing items, and unusual activity before prep starts

low.headline: Prep is where your margin dies
low.text: When every preparer works differently and data is rekeyed, you are paying twice: once in prep hours and once in review hours catching inconsistencies. Standardize before you automate.
steps: Pick one engagement type (1040, monthly close, audit) and document the canonical workpaper structure for it
Roll out OCR plus structured extraction for your three highest-volume document types first
Replace email-based document chasing with an automated reminder workflow tied to your portal

mid.headline: Good bones, leaky execution
mid.text: Your standards exist but adherence is uneven and humans are still doing low-value reconciliation. The win is moving humans from data entry to exception handling.
steps: Audit which document types still hit a human keyboard and rank them by annual volume
Layer AI extraction on top of OCR with confidence thresholds — humans only see flagged items
Build a rollforward template that auto-flags variances above a threshold against prior year

high.headline: Keep sharpening — make the system catch what reviewers do
high.text: You have eliminated most of the manual prep load. The frontier is moving review-tier judgment earlier, so issues get caught at prep, not at partner review.
steps: Train the extraction layer to flag the specific anomalies your reviewers catch most often
Add a pre-review automated quality pass that blocks workpapers from advancing until checks pass
Build cross-engagement intelligence — flag when a client's pattern this year diverges from their three-year baseline

questions.text: How consistently does every reviewer use the same checklist and sign-off structure?

answers.label: 1 — Reviewers use their own judgment with no formal checklist
2 — A checklist exists in a shared doc but is rarely opened
3 — Checklists are used per engagement type, enforcement varies by reviewer
4 — Checklists are mandatory and tracked per engagement
5 — Workflow blocks engagement progression until each checklist item is signed off

questions.text: How often does work sit in a partner or senior manager review queue for more than 48 hours?

answers.label: 1 — Constantly — review is the firm's permanent bottleneck
2 — Frequently during busy season
3 — Sometimes, on complex engagements
4 — Rarely — review SLAs are tracked and mostly hit
5 — Almost never — review queues are visible, prioritized, and managed in real time

questions.text: How systematically are review notes captured, resolved, and learned from across engagements?

answers.label: 1 — Verbal feedback or scattered email threads
2 — Notes live in the workpaper, no firm-wide visibility
3 — Logged per engagement, occasionally reviewed in team meetings
4 — Centrally tracked with categorized issue types and resolution status
5 — Pattern analysis feeds back into prep training and checklist updates quarterly

low.headline: Review is the firm's silent partner tax
low.text: When every reviewer uses their own judgment and notes live in their head, you pay for the same mistakes every year. The first fix is making review consistent before making it faster.
steps: Document the review checklist for your top 2 engagement types and make it the only path to sign-off
Track review queue age in a shared dashboard so bottlenecks are visible, not anecdotal
Capture every review note in a structured log so patterns surface across engagements

mid.headline: Review is structured, not yet leveraged
mid.text: You catch most issues, but partners are still the bottleneck and learnings are not feeding back into prep. Move from catching mistakes to preventing them.
steps: Categorize review notes by issue type for one quarter to find your top 5 recurring leaks
Push the top recurring issues upstream into prep checklists and automated quality checks
Set a hard SLA on review queue age and surface breaches to leadership weekly

high.headline: Keep sharpening — turn review into a learning loop
high.text: You have controls. The frontier is using review data to continuously upgrade your prep layer so reviewers spend time on judgment, not on catching the same issues every year.
steps: Run a quarterly issue-pattern analysis and update the prep checklist and automated checks accordingly
Tier engagements by review complexity so partner time goes to the engagements that need it
Use review issue rates per preparer as a leading indicator for training, not just performance

questions.text: How consistently do clients know where their engagement stands without having to email and ask?

answers.label: 1 — They email us — we then check internally and reply
2 — Manual status updates sent at major milestones
3 — Portal shows status, but it is updated by hand
4 — Status auto-updates as work progresses, clients see it live
5 — Proactive notifications go out at every milestone, no client has to ask

questions.text: How automated is the routing and first-response on inbound client emails and portal messages?

answers.label: 1 — Everything lands in a shared inbox and someone triages by hand
2 — Filters and rules sort some, the rest is manual
3 — Routing is automated, responses are still fully manual
4 — Routine inquiries get templated auto-responses with human review
5 — AI-drafted replies for routine asks, humans approve and send in seconds

questions.text: How proactively does the firm reach out to clients between engagements (deadlines, planning windows, regulatory changes)?

answers.label: 1 — Only when the client reaches out first
2 — Annual reminder for the main filing, nothing else
3 — A handful of scheduled touchpoints per client per year
4 — Segmented cadence by client type with automated triggers
5 — Every client gets event-driven outreach tied to their specific situation and deadlines

low.headline: Clients are running your inbox
low.text: Every status email a client sends costs the firm 5–10 minutes of someone checking, drafting, and replying. Multiply by your client count and the leak is real. Stop letting clients pull status from you.
steps: Pick one engagement type and add a client-visible status that updates automatically as work moves stages
Send proactive milestone notifications so clients stop emailing for updates
Set up auto-routing on your shared inbox so triage stops eating senior time

mid.headline: Comms are reactive, not yet proactive
mid.text: You answer clients well, but they still drive the pace. Move from responsive to proactive — it changes how clients perceive the firm and frees significant senior-team capacity.
steps: Build a client-facing status view fed by your PM system so updates do not require human work
Layer AI-drafted first-response on routine inquiries with a human approve-and-send step
Define a proactive touchpoint cadence per client segment and automate the triggers

high.headline: Keep sharpening — turn comms into advisory leverage
high.text: You handle volume well. The frontier is using your data to deliver client-specific insight, not just status — that is what makes a boutique firm impossible to leave.
steps: Trigger personalized outreach on client-specific events (deadline windows, threshold crossings, life events)
Send quarterly client-specific advisory snapshots auto-generated from their data
Score client engagement health and flag at-risk relationships before they churn

questions.text: How accurately does time tracking reflect actual work, and how cleanly does it flow into billing?

answers.label: 1 — Reconstructed from memory at month-end, billing is a fight
2 — Tracked weekly, frequent corrections at billing time
3 — Tracked daily, billing is mostly clean with manual review
4 — Captured at task level, billing is automated with exception review
5 — Auto-captured from system activity and flows straight to billing with no rework

questions.text: How clearly can leadership see, in real time, who has capacity this week and where bottlenecks are forming?

answers.label: 1 — We ask people in standup or guess from inboxes
2 — Spreadsheet updated weekly by an admin
3 — PM tool shows assigned work, capacity load is interpreted by hand
4 — Live dashboard of utilization and queue depth per person
5 — Capacity-aware system that auto-routes work and flags overloads before they hit

questions.text: How accessible are the firm's SOPs, decisions, and how-tos to a new hire on day one?

answers.label: 1 — In partners' heads — new hires shadow until they figure it out
2 — Scattered docs across email, drives, and the PM tool
3 — Centralized in one tool, but updates lag and search is poor
4 — Living SOP library with ownership and review cadence
5 — Searchable knowledge base plus AI assistant trained on firm-specific procedures

low.headline: You cannot scale what you cannot see
low.text: If capacity lives in people's heads and SOPs live in partners' heads, every hire costs you a quarter of someone's time to onboard. This is the foundation under everything else — fix it before automating anywhere else.
steps: Pick one tool to be the single source of truth for time, tasks, and capacity — and make it mandatory
Document your top 10 most-asked-about procedures into a living SOP library this quarter
Add a weekly capacity view leadership actually opens before assigning new work

mid.headline: Tooling exists, instrumentation is partial
mid.text: You have systems, but they are not yet feeding leadership the signal needed to make capacity calls in real time. Tighten the loop between work, time, and capacity visibility.
steps: Replace manual time entry with system-captured activity where possible — meet preparers where they work
Build a leadership dashboard that shows queue depth, utilization, and at-risk engagements live
Assign owners and quarterly review cycles to every SOP so the library stays current

high.headline: Keep sharpening — make the system route work, not the partners
high.text: Your visibility is strong. The frontier is letting capacity data drive routing decisions automatically, so partners stop being the firm's allocation engine.
steps: Auto-route incoming work based on real-time capacity, skill match, and engagement priority
Layer an AI assistant on your SOP library so any team member can self-serve firm-specific guidance
Tie capacity forecasts to your sales pipeline so you sign clients when capacity exists, not when it does not

ctas.headline: Want a personalized Loom breakdown of your biggest workflow leak?
ctas.description: Drop your email and I'll review your exact answers, stack them against accounting firms your size, and send a 5-minute Loom within 48h with the ONE fix I'd prioritize first. Async, no scheduling pain, no pitch. If we both see signal, we book a working call from there. This is how you scale without scaling payroll — find the leak, plug the leak.

ctas.headline: Under 10 staff? You're early for a custom audit.
ctas.description: Automation ROI is real at your scale, but the 90-Day Payback Rule is harder to hit until you cross ~10 staff. Follow me on LinkedIn — I post tactical accounting ops breakdowns 3x/week. When you cross 11 staff and have repeat workflows worth automating, come back and retake; the CTA will change.

ctas.headline: You're running tight — here's what accounting firms your size miss next.
ctas.description: At your score, the obvious leaks are closed. The next gains come from pre-engagement triage (saying no faster), cross-engagement intelligence (using prior-year data to catch issues before review), and capacity-aware sales (signing when capacity exists, not when it doesn't). I'm writing teardowns on each — follow along.

ctas.headline: Get the weekly accounting ops teardown
ctas.description: One tactical case study + one n8n workflow breakdown per week. How mid-market accounting firms run lean and Hire a System instead of another junior — no fluff, no pitch.
